blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
By the Manifest Book! (2)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنذِرِينَ (٣)
We sent it down on a blessed night, and We have been warning [mankind]. (3)
فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ (٤)
Every definitive matter is resolved on it, (4)
أَمْرًا مِّنْ عِندِنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (٥)
as an ordinance from Us. We have been sending [apostles] (5)
رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦)
as a mercy from your Lord—indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing— (6)
رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (٧)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, should you have conviction. (7)
لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
There is no god except Him: He gives life and brings death, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers. (8)

thoughtful

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is summarized in warning those who doubt the Book regarding the punishment of this world and the punishment of the Hereafter. This is presented by explaining that it is a manifest Book revealed from Allah to the one He sent to the people to warn them. It was revealed as a mercy from Him, the Exalted, to His servants in the best of descents during the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), in which every precise matter is made distinct.

However, the people—namely the disbelievers—doubted it, playing about in their whims. The painful punishment of this world shall soon envelop them, then they shall return to their Lord, and He will take vengeance upon them after the final judgment with an eternal punishment.

Then, as a parallel to the first of the two threats, He mentions the story of the sending of Musa (عليه السلام) to the people of Pharaoh to deliver the Children of Israel, and their denial of him, and their subsequent drowning as an exemplary punishment from Him.

Next, He mentions their denial of the second threat, which is the return to Allah on the Day of Distinction (يوم الفصل). He establishes the proof that it is coming without doubt, then mentions some of its reports and what will occur therein to the guilty (المجرمين) and the various types of punishment that will afflict them, as well as the pleasant life and honorable station with which the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the innate protective shield of the soul against sin and divine displeasure — will be rewarded.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: حم وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ Ha, Meem. By the Manifest Book! The waw is for an oath, and what is meant by the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) is the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night, and indeed We have been warning [mankind]. What is meant by the "blessed night" (ليلة مباركة) in which the Quran was revealed is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) — the night of decree and majesty in which the affairs of the year are determined — as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment (97:1). Its being "blessed" (مباركة) refers to its being a vessel for the abundant good that spreads over creation from the vast mercy, as the Exalted has said: وَما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment? The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months (97:3).

The literal sense of the wording is that it is one of the nights that rotate upon the earth. The literal sense of His saying: فِيها يُفْرَقُ In it is made distinct..., which indicates continuity, is that it recurs. The literal sense of His saying, the Exalted: شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ The month of Ramaḍān is one in which the Quran was sent down (2:185), is that it recurs with the recurrence of the month of Ramadan. Thus, it recurs with the recurrence of the lunar years and occurs once in every lunar year during the month of Ramadan. As for which specific night it is, there is no indication of that in the speech of the Exalted; however, the narrations (الروايات) will be presented to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions."

What is meant by the revelation of the Book in a blessed night—according to the literal sense of His saying: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ and His saying: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ (97:1) and His saying: شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْهُدى وَالْفُرْقانِ The month of Ramaḍān is one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance to mankind, with manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion (2:185)—is that what was revealed was the entire Quran.

This is not contradicted by His saying: وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً As a Quran We have divided it into parts that you may read it to the people leisurely, and We have sent it down piecemeal (17:106), nor by His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَرَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلاً The faithless say, ‘Why has not the Quran been sent down to him all at once?’ So it is, that We may strengthen your heart with it, and We have recited it in a measured tone (25:32), both of which literally suggest its gradual revelation (النزول التدريجي). This is further supported by other verses such as His saying: فَإِذا أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُحْكَمَةٌ But when a definitive surah is sent down... (47:20), and His saying: وَإِذا ما أُنْزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ نَظَرَ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى بَعْضٍ Whenever a surah is sent down, they look at one another... (9:127), and others. It is also supported by countless reports containing the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

This is because it can be interpreted as the Quran being revealed twice: once collectively and as a whole (جملة) in a single night of the nights of the month of Ramadan, and once gradually and in stages (نجوما) over the period of twenty-three years, which was the duration of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) mission.

However, that which should not be doubted is that this Quran, composed of Surahs and verses with its various contexts (السياقات) corresponding to specific and personal occasions of revelation, does not admit of being revealed all at once. For the verses revealed regarding personal events and particular incidents (الحوادث الجزئية) linked to specific times, places, persons, and circumstances are only true upon the realization of their various occasions, which are scattered in time and space. If they were gathered in one time and place, they would deviate from those occasions and become something else. Thus, it is impossible to conceive of the revelation of the Quran, in its exact form and state, once as a whole and once in stages.

Therefore, if it is said to have been revealed twice, it is necessary to distinguish between the two revelations by way of brevity (الإجمال) and detail (التفصيل). Thus, it would have been revealed once in a concise, non-manifest state and once in a detailed, manifest state. By this brevity and detail, we mean what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ [This is] a Book whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated from the presence of One who is All-wise, All-aware (11:1), and His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise (43:3-4). The discussion regarding the meaning of "making definitive" (الإحكام) and "elaboration" (التفصيل) has already passed in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Hud and Surah al-Zukhruf.

It has been said: What is meant by the revelation of the Book in a blessed night is the commencement of its gradual revelation on the Night of Ordainment in the month of Ramadan. Thus, the first of the verses of the Quran to be revealed—which is Surah al-Alaq or Surah al-Hamd—was revealed on the Night of Ordainment.

This view is based on a perceived contradiction between the revelation of the entire Book in one night and its gradual revelation indicated by the previous verses. You have already seen that there is no contradiction between the verses. Furthermore, as you are aware, this is contrary to the literal sense of the verses.

It has also been said: It was first revealed as a whole to the lowest heaven (السماء الدنيا) on the Night of Ordainment, then it was revealed from the lowest heaven to the earth gradually over twenty-three years, the duration of the Prophetic mission. This view is taken from the reports narrated regarding the commentary of the verses that literally suggest its revelation as a whole, and they will be presented to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ ...and indeed We have been warning [mankind] stands in the position of a causal explanation (التعليل). It indicates the continuity of warning from Him, the Exalted, prior to this warning. This shows that the revelation of the Quran from Him, the Exalted, is not an unprecedented thing (بدع); rather, it is a warning, and warning is an established sunnah (سنة جارية) of His that has always been carried out among the predecessors through the path of revelation (الوحي) to the prophets and messengers and their being sent to warn the people.

His saying, the Exalted: فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ In it is made distinct every precise matter. The pronoun in "in it" (فِيها) refers to the night. "Distinguishing" (الفرق) is the separation of one thing from another such that they become distinct (يتمايزان), and its opposite is "making definitive" (الإحكام). Thus, a "precise matter" (الأمر الحكيم) is that whose parts are not distinguished from one another, nor are its specificities and states determined, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure (15:21).

Thus, for all matters, according to the Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي), there are two stages: the stage of brevity and ambiguity (الإجمال والإبهام) and the stage of detail (التفصيل). The Night of Ordainment—as indicated by His saying: فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ—is a night in which matters emerge from the stage of being definitive (الإحكام) to the stage of distinction (الفرق) and detail. The Quran was revealed in it, and it is one of the definitive matters that was made distinct on the Night of Ordainment.

Perhaps Allah, the Glorified, informed His Prophet of the details of the events that would occur during the time of his mission and what would coincide with the revelation of each verse, verses, or Surah of His Book, thereby necessitating their revelation. He informed him of what would be revealed of it, so the Quran was revealed to him all at once and as a whole before its gradual and fragmented revelation.

The outcome of this view is the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) awareness of the Quran at the stage of its descent to the detailed decree (القضاء التفصيلي) before its descent to the earth and its establishment in the stage of external reality (العين). According to this view, there is no need to distinguish the two revelations by brevity and detail as mentioned in the first view.

The literal sense of the speech of some is that what is meant by His saying: فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ is the detailing of the matters explained in the Quran, such as divine gnosis (معارف), laws (أحكام), and so on. This is refuted by the fact that the literal sense of His saying: فِيها يُفْرَقُ indicates continuity (الاستمرار). That which continues in this night through its recurrence is the detailing of cosmic matters (الأمور الكونية) after their being definitive. As for the divine gnosis and laws, there is no continuity in their detailing; if their distinction were intended, it would have been more appropriate to say: "in it was made distinct" (فيها فرق).

It has been said: What is meant by the matter being "precise" (حكيم) is its being made definitive after the distinction, not the definitiveness that precedes the detailing. The meaning would be: every definitive matter that does not change by increase, decrease, or otherwise is decreed in the night. However, the most manifest meaning is what we have previously presented.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ ...as a command from Us. Indeed We have been sending [messengers]. What is meant by "command" (الأمر) here is "affair" (الشأن), and it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the previous "matter" (الأمر). The meaning is: in it every matter is made distinct while being an affair from Us and originating from Our presence. It is also possible that what is meant by it is the opposite of "prohibition," meaning: every matter is made distinct by a command from Us. In any case, it is connected to His saying: يُفْرَقُ (is made distinct).

It is also possible that it is connected to His saying: أَنْزَلْناهُ (We sent it down), meaning: while the Book is a command or by a command from Us. His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ is not without support for this, serving as a causal explanation for it. The meaning would be: We sent it down as a command from Us because Our established sunnah is the sending of prophets and messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ ...as a mercy from your Lord. Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. That is, its revelation is a mercy from your Lord, or We revealed it for the sake of bestowing mercy upon the people, or the mercy of your Lord necessitated its revelation. Thus, His saying: "mercy" (رَحْمَةً) is a circumstantial qualifier according to the first meaning, and an object of purpose (مفعول له) according to the second and third.

In His saying: مِنْ رَبِّكَ (from your Lord), there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural to the third person. The reason for this is to manifest the care for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), because he is the one to whom the Quran was revealed and he is the warner sent to the people.

His saying: إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ means: the One who hears the requests and knows the needs. Thus, He hears their request and knows their need for being guided by the guidance of your Lord, so He reveals the Book and sends the Messenger as a mercy from Him to them.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, should you have certainty. Since the idolaters (الوثنية) believed that every category of creation had a god or more, and sometimes a group among them took a god other than what others took, He followed His saying: مِنْ رَبِّكَ with His saying: رَبِّ السَّماواتِ etc., so that none of them would imagine that His Lordship (ربوبيته) over the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a specialized one like that which exists among them. Rather, He, the Exalted, is his Lord and the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. For this reason, He also followed it in the next verse with His saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ There is no god but He.

His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ (should you have certainty)—this condition, as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari, is like our saying: "This is the favor of Zayd, whose generosity people have heard of and whose liberality is famous, if his report has reached you and you have been told his story." Thus, the meaning is: He is the one whom those who possess certainty (الموقنون) recognize as the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them; if you are among them, you would recognize Him as the Lord of everything.

His saying, the Exalted: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ There is no god but He: He gives life and brings death, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers. Since the implication of the previous verse was the restriction of Lordship (الربوبية)—which is ownership and governance (الملك والتدبير)—to Him, the Exalted, and since Divinity (الألوهية)—which is being the rightful object of worship—is among the necessary concomitants of Lordship, He followed it with the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد) which negates every god besides Him, the Exalted.

His saying: يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ (He gives life and brings death) are among the most specific attributes belonging to Him, the Exalted, and they are among the affairs of governance. In mentioning them, there is a kind of preparation for what is to come regarding the warning of the Return (المعاد).

His saying: رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ (your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers) contains a complete explicit declaration that He is their Lord and the Lord of their fathers; so let them worship Him and not make excuses by following their fathers in the worship of idols. To complete the explicit declaration, the sentence was presented in the second-person address, so it was said: "your Lord and the Lord of your fathers" (رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ آبائِكُمُ).

These two—namely His saying: يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ and His saying: رَبُّكُمْ—are predicates (خبران) for a suppressed subject, the estimation being: "He (هو) gives life and brings death..." etc.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ: "The blessed night is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (عليهما السلام)."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from 'Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from 'Umar ibn Udhaynah, from al-Fudayl, Zurarah, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Humran, that he asked Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ. He said: نعم ليلة القدر وهي في كل سنة في شهر رمضان ـ في العشر الأواخر ـ فلم ينزل القرآن إلا في ليلة القدر ـ قال الله تعالى : « فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ » قال : يقدر في ليلة القدر كل شيء ـ يكون في تلك السنة إلى مثلها من قابل : خير وشر وطاعة ومعصية ومولود وأجل ورزق ـ فما قدر في تلك السنة وقضي فهو المحتوم ـ ولله تعالى فيه المشية. "Yes, it is the Night of Ordainment, and it is in every year in the month of Ramadan—in the last ten nights. The Quran was not revealed except on the Night of Ordainment. Allah, the Exalted, said: 'In it is made distinct every precise matter.' He said: 'Everything that will be in that year until the same time in the following year is ordained on the Night of Ordainment: good and evil, obedience and disobedience, births, appointed terms (آجال), and sustenance (رزق). So whatever is ordained in that year and decreed is the definitive (المحتوم), and for Allah, the Exalted, therein is the Will (المشية).'"

I say: His saying: "it is the definitive, and for Allah therein is the Will" means that it is definitive from the perspective of causes and conditions, so nothing prevents its realization unless Allah so wills.

In al-Basa'ir, from 'Abbas ibn Ma'ruf, from Sa'dan ibn Muslim, from 'Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I asked him about the middle of Sha'ban, and he said: ما عندي فيه شيء ـ ولكن إذا كانت ليلة تسع عشرة من شهر رمضان ـ قسم فيها الأرزاق وكتب فيها الآجال ـ وخرج فيها صكاك الحاج واطلع الله إلى عباده ـ فغفر الله لهم إلا شارب خمر مسكر. فإذا كانت ليلة ثلاث وعشرين فيها يفرق كل أمر حكيم ـ ثم ينهى ذلك ويمضي ذلك. 'I have nothing regarding it. However, when it is the nineteenth night of the month of Ramadan, the sustenances are apportioned, the appointed terms are written, the documents of the pilgrims are issued, and Allah looks upon His servants and forgives them, except for the drinker of intoxicating wine. Then, when it is the twenty-third night, in it every precise matter is made distinct; then it is concluded and executed.'" I asked: "To whom?" He said: إلى صاحبكم ولو لا ذلك لم يعلم. "To your companion [the Imam]; were it not for that, he would not know."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Muhammad ibn Nasr, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn 'Abbas regarding His saying, the Exalted: فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ. He said: يكتب من أم الكتاب في ليلة القدر ما يكون في السنة ـ من رزق أو موت أو حياة أو مطر ـ حتى يكتب الحاج : يحج فلان ويحج فلان. "There is written from the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) on the Night of Ordainment whatever will be in the year—of sustenance, death, life, or rain—even the pilgrims are written: so-and-so will perform the Hajj, and so-and-so will perform the Hajj."

I say: The reports regarding the Night of Ordainment, what is decreed therein, and its identification are very numerous, and the most important of them will be presented in the commentary of Surah al-Qadr, Allah, the Exalted, willing.

Ayahs

بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩)
But they play around in doubt. (9)
فَٱرْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِدُخَانٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٠)
So watch out for the day when the sky brings on a manifest smoke, (10)
يَغْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ ۖ هَـٰذَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١١)
enveloping the people. [They will cry out:] ‘This is a painful punishment. (11)
رَّبَّنَا ٱكْشِفْ عَنَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢)
Our Lord! Remove this punishment from us. Indeed we have believed!’ (12)
أَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ وَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٣)
What will the admonition avail them, when a manifest apostle had already come to them, (13)
ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ (١٤)
but they turned away from him, and said, ‘A tutored madman?’ (14)
إِنَّا كَاشِفُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابِ قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ عَآئِدُونَ (١٥)
Indeed We will withdraw the punishment a little; but you will revert [to your earlier ways]. (15)
يَوْمَ نَبْطِشُ ٱلْبَطْشَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ إِنَّا مُنتَقِمُونَ (١٦)
The day We shall strike with the most terrible striking, We will indeed take vengeance [on them]. (16)
۞ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَجَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ (١٧)
Certainly We tried the people of Pharaoh before them, when a noble apostle came to them, (17)
أَنْ أَدُّوٓا۟ إِلَىَّ عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٨)
[saying,] ‘Give over the servants of Allah to me; indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (18)
وَأَن لَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٩)
Do not defy Allah. Indeed I bring you a manifest authority. (19)
وَإِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُمْ أَن تَرْجُمُونِ (٢٠)
I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord, lest you should stone me. (20)
وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ لِى فَٱعْتَزِلُونِ (٢١)
And if you do not believe me, keep out of my way.’ (21)
فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ مُّجْرِمُونَ (٢٢)
Then he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘These are indeed a guilty lot.’ (22)
فَأَسْرِ بِعِبَادِى لَيْلًا إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ (٢٣)
[Allah told him,] ‘Set out by night with My servants; for you will be pursued. (23)
وَٱتْرُكِ ٱلْبَحْرَ رَهْوًا ۖ إِنَّهُمْ جُندٌ مُّغْرَقُونَ (٢٤)
Leave behind the sea unmoving; for they will be a drowned host.’ (24)
كَمْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٢٥)
How many gardens and springs did they leave behind! (25)
وَزُرُوعٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ (٢٦)
Fields and splendid places, (26)
وَنَعْمَةٍ كَانُوا۟ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهِينَ (٢٧)
and the affluence wherein they rejoiced! (27)
كَذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٢٨)
So it was; and We bequeathed them to another people. (28)
فَمَا بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنظَرِينَ (٢٩)
So neither the sky wept for them, nor the earth; nor were they granted any respite. (29)
وَلَقَدْ نَجَّيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ (٣٠)
Certainly We delivered the Children of Israel from the humiliating torment (30)
مِن فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَالِيًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣١)
of Pharaoh. Indeed, he was a tyrant among the transgressors. (31)
وَلَقَدِ ٱخْتَرْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٢)
Certainly We chose them knowingly above all the nations, (32)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ مَا فِيهِ بَلَـٰٓؤٌا۟ مُّبِينٌ (٣٣)
and We gave them some signs in which there was a manifest test. (33)

(Commentary)

The verses mention their doubt regarding the Book of Allah after having stated that it is a manifest Book, revealed on a blessed night to His Messenger for the purpose of warning, as a mercy from Allah. Then, the verses threaten them with the punishment of this world and the [mighty] seizure of the Day of Resurrection, presenting to them the story of the sending of Moses to the people of Pharaoh, their denial of him, and their subsequent drowning.

The story is not devoid of an allusion that He, the Exalted, will save the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers with him from the tyrants of the Quraysh by bringing them out of Mecca, and then destroying the chieftains of the Quraysh as they pursue the Prophet and the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ — But they are in doubt, playing around. (44:9). The plural pronoun refers to the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The transition (الإضراب) is from a suppressed clause indicated by the preceding context; meaning: they do not have certainty nor do they believe in what has been mentioned regarding the mission of the Messenger and the description of the Book revealed to him; rather, they are in doubt and suspicion regarding it, playing around by occupying themselves with their worldly life. Al-Zamakhshari mentioned that the transition is from His saying: "if you have certainty."

His saying, the Exalted: فارْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِي السَّماءُ بِدُخانٍ مُبِينٍ يَغْشَى النَّاسَ — So watch out for the day when the sky brings forth a manifest smoke, enshrouding the people. (44:10-11). Watching out (الارتقاب) means waiting (الانتظار). This is a threat of punishment, which is the sky bringing forth a manifest smoke that enshrouds the people.

There is a difference of opinion regarding the intended meaning of this punishment mentioned in the verse.

It is said: It refers to the famine that afflicted the people of Mecca. When they persisted in their disbelief and their harm to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed against them, saying: "O Allah, [send upon them] years like the years of Joseph." Consequently, the earth became barren and a severe famine struck the Quraysh. Due to hunger, a man would see something like smoke between himself and the sky, and they ate carrion and bones. Then they came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Muhammad, you have come commanding the joining of kinship, yet your people have perished." They promised him that if Allah removed the drought from them, they would believe. So he prayed and asked Allah for fertility and abundance for them, and He removed it from them; however, they returned to their disbelief and broke their covenant.

It is also said: The smoke mentioned in the verse is one of the signs of the Hour (أشراط الساعة) and it has not yet occurred. It will come before the establishment of the Hour and enter the ears of the people until their heads become like a roasted head (الحنيذ).

The believer will be affected by it like a common cold, and the entire earth will be like a house in which a fire has been lit and which has no apertures (خصاص) — small holes or openings — and it will remain for forty days.

It has also been said: The "Day of Smoke" refers to the day of the Conquest of Mecca, when the Muslim army entered Mecca and the dust rose like dark smoke. It has also been said: It refers to the Day of Resurrection. Both views are as you see [unlikely].

His saying: يَغْشَى النَّاسَ — enshrouding the people — means it covers them and encompasses them. "The people" refers to the people of Mecca according to the first view, and all of humanity according to the second view.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ رَبَّنَا اكْشِفْ عَنَّا الْعَذابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ — ‘This is a painful punishment.’ ‘Our Lord! Remove this punishment from us, for we are indeed believers!’ (44:11-12). This is a narration of what the people will say when the punishment of the smoke descends. That is, the people will say on the day the sky brings forth a manifest smoke: "This is a painful punishment," and they will ask Allah to remove it by acknowledging His Lordship and manifesting faith in the true call, saying: رَبَّنَا اكْشِفْ عَنَّا الْعَذابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنَّى لَهُمُ الذِّكْرى وَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُبِينٌ — How will they take admonition, when a manifest messenger has already come to them, (44:13). Meaning: how is it possible for them to remember and submit to the truth when a manifest messenger, clear in his message and beyond suspicion, has already come to them—and he is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)? The verse is a rejection of the truthfulness of their promise.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهَ وَقالُوا مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ — then they turned away from him and said, ‘[He is] a tutored madman.’ (44:14). Turning away (التولي) is aversion (الإعراض). The pronoun in عَنْهُ refers to the Messenger. مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ are two predicates for a suppressed subject, which is a pronoun referring to the Messenger. The meaning is: then they turned away from the Messenger and said: "He is a tutored madman." They accused him first of being "tutored" (مُعَلَّمٌ)—meaning someone else teaches him and he attributes what he learns to Allah, the Glorified; as He, the Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ — We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human who teaches him.’ (16:103). Secondly, they accused him of being "mad" (مَجْنُونٌ)—meaning mentally deranged.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا كاشِفُوا الْعَذابِ قَلِيلاً إِنَّكُمْ عائِدُونَ — Indeed We will withdraw the punishment for a while, [but] you will indeed revert [to your old ways]. (44:15). Meaning: We shall remove the punishment for a short time, yet you will return to the disbelief and denial you were in. This is based on the first view, and the verse is a confirmation of the rejection of their truthfulness in the faith they promised.

As for the second view, the most likely meaning is: you will return to the punishment on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَبْطِشُ الْبَطْشَةَ الْكُبْرى إِنَّا مُنْتَقِمُونَ — The day We shall strike with the most terrible power, We will indeed take vengeance [on them]. (44:16). Seizing (البطش)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is to take something with force (صولة). This day, based on the first aforementioned view, is the Day of Badr, and based on the second view, it is the Day of Resurrection. The description of the seizure as "the greatest" (الكبرى) may support this second view, for the seizure and punishment of the Day of Resurrection is the greatest seizure and punishment. He, the Exalted, said: فَيُعَذِّبُهُ اللهُ الْعذابَ الْأَكْبَرَ — then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment. (88:24), just as its reward is the greatest reward; He, the Exalted, said: وَلَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ — and the reward of the Hereafter is surely greater, (16:41).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَجاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ — Certainly We tried the people of Pharaoh before them, and a noble messenger came to them, (44:17). Trial (الفتنة) is testing (الامتحان) and tribulation (الابتلاء) to reach the reality of a thing. His saying: وَجاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ etc., is an explanation of the test. The noble messenger is Moses (عليه‌السلام). The noble (الكريم) is one characterized by praiseworthy qualities. Al-Raghib said: "When 'nobility' (الكرم) is used to describe Allah, the Exalted, it is a name for His manifest beneficence and favor, such as His saying: فَإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ — for my Lord is indeed All-sufficient, All-generous. (27:40). When it describes a human, it is a name for the praiseworthy character and actions that manifest from him; one is not called 'noble' until that manifests from him." He continued: "And everything that is honorable in its kind is described with nobility (الكرم); He, the Exalted, said: أَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ — We have made every kind of noble [plant] grow in it? (26:7), وَزُرُوعٍ وَمَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ — and cornfields and noble abodes, (44:26), إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ — This is indeed a noble Quran, (56:77), and وَقُلْ لَهُما قَوْلاً كَرِيماً — and speak to them with noble words. (17:23)." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنْ أَدُّوا إِلَيَّ عِبادَ اللهِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ — [saying,] ‘Deliver the servants of Allah to me. Indeed I am a trusted messenger to you. (44:18). This is an explanation of the "coming" of the Messenger, for the meaning of the Messenger's coming is the delivery of the message. Part of the message of Moses (عليه‌السلام) to Pharaoh and his people was to release the Children of Israel with them and not to punish them. The "servants of Allah" (عباد الله) refers to the Children of Israel; he referred to them as such to evoke mercy and to hint that in their arrogance and transgression against them, they were only being arrogant toward Allah because they are the servants of Allah.

In his saying: إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ, where he described himself with trustworthiness (الأمانة), he repelled the possibility that he might betray them in his claim of messengership or in saving the Children of Israel from their control—that he might go out with them against them to expel them from their land, as Allah narrated from Pharaoh when he said to the elite around him: إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ بِسِحْرِهِ — ‘This is indeed a knowing magician! He wishes to expel you from your land with his magic.’ (26:34-35).

It is said: عِبادَ اللهِ is a vocative for Pharaoh and his people, and the estimation is: "Deliver to me what I command you, O servants of Allah." This is not devoid of an estimation that contradicts the apparent meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ لا تَعْلُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِنِّي آتِيكُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ — And do not be arrogant toward Allah. Indeed I bring you a manifest authority. (44:19). Meaning: do not be tyrannical toward Allah by denying my message and turning away from what Allah has commanded you, for denying the Messenger in his message is an act of exaltation and tyranny against the One who sent him. The evidence for this meaning is the justification of the prohibition with His saying: إِنِّي آتِيكُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ, meaning a prominent proof from the miraculous signs, or the proof of the miracle and the proof of demonstration.

It is said: Among the beauties of the expression is the pairing of "delivery" (التأدية) with "trustworthy" (الأمين), and likewise between "arrogance" (العلو) and "authority" (السلطان).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ أَنْ تَرْجُمُونِ — I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord lest you should stone me. (44:20). Meaning: I have taken refuge in Him, the Exalted, from your stoning me, so you will not be able to do that. It apparently refers to the security his Lord granted him before coming to the people, as in His saying, the Exalted: قالا رَبَّنا إِنَّنا نَخافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنا أَوْ أَنْ يَطْغى قالَ لا تَخافا إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى — They said, ‘Our Lord! We fear that he will overbear us or act tyrannically.’ He said, ‘Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see.’ (20:45-46).

From what has passed, the invalidity of the claim that this occurred before Allah informed him of their inability to stone him with His saying: فَلا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُما — so they will not reach you. (28:35) becomes clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ لَمْ تُؤْمِنُوا لِي فَاعْتَزِلُونِ — And if you do not believe me, then leave me alone.’ (44:21). Meaning: if you do not believe in me, then remain apart from me—neither for me nor against me—and do not interfere with me for good or for evil. It is said: the meaning is "depart from me and break away," but this is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنَّ هؤُلاءِ قَوْمٌ مُجْرِمُونَ — Then he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘These are a guilty people.’ (44:22). Meaning: he called upon Him [stating] that these are a guilty people. He mentioned in his prayer the reason that prompted him to pray, which was their criminality to a degree that they deserved destruction. What he asked for is known from the response of his Lord, the Exalted, when He said: فَأَسْرِ بِعِبادِي etc., which is their destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَسْرِ بِعِبادِي لَيْلاً إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ — ‘Depart with My servants by night, for you will be pursued. (44:23). Night-journeying (الإسراء) is traveling by night, so His saying: لَيْلاً — by night — is an emphasis and an explicit mention of it. "My servants" refers to the Children of Israel. His saying: إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ — for you will be pursued — means Pharaoh and his soldiers will follow you. This is an initial sentence (استئناف) informing of what will occur following the night-journey.

In the speech, there is a conciseness through omission (إيجاز بالحذف), and the estimation is: "So He said to him: Depart with My servants by night, for you will be pursued; Pharaoh and his soldiers will follow you."

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً إِنَّهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُغْرَقُونَ — And leave the sea still; they are indeed a host that is to be drowned.’ (44:24). It is said in al-Mufradat: "وَاتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً means still (ساكنا). It is also said: a wide path, and this is the correct view." End quote. His saying: إِنَّهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُغْرَقُونَ is the justification for His saying: وَاتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً.

In the speech, there is a conciseness through omission for the sake of brevity, and the estimation is: "Depart with My servants by night; Pharaoh and his soldiers will follow you until you reach the sea. Then strike it with your staff so that a path opens for your passage. Cross it and leave it still or open as it is, so they will enter it out of greed to catch you, for they are a host to be drowned."

His saying, the Exalted: كَمْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَزُرُوعٍ وَمَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ وَنَعْمَةٍ كانُوا فِيها فاكِهِينَ كَذلِكَ — How many gardens and springs did they leave behind, and cornfields and noble abodes, and the luxuries in which they used to take delight! So it was. (44:25-28). كَمْ is for multiplicity, meaning: how many things they left behind. His saying: مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ etc., is an explanation of what they left. The "noble abode" (المقام الكريم) refers to beautiful and splendid dwellings. Luxury (النعمة) with a fathah on the nun means the act of enjoying luxury (التنعم), and its structure is that of a single instance (بناء المرة) like "a strike" (الضربة). With a kasrah on the nun, it is a type of enjoying luxury, and its structure is that of a kind (بناء النوع) like "a way of sitting" (الجلسة). They have interpreted "luxury" (النعمة) here as that which is enjoyed, which is more appropriate for the context of "leaving behind." فاكِهِينَ — taking delight — is from al-fakahah, meaning pleasant conversation; perhaps what is intended here is enjoyment, just as one enjoys fruits (الفواكه), which are types of produce.

Regarding His saying: كَذلِكَ — So it was — it is said: its meaning is "the matter was thus." It is also said: the meaning is "We do such an act to those whom We intend to destroy." It is also said: the reference is to the "expulsion" understood from the previous speech, and the meaning is: "Like that expulsion, We expelled them from it."

It is possible that it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the omitted object of "they left" (تركوا), and the meaning is: "How many things they left in such a state," i.e., as they were. Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْرَثْناها قَوْماً آخَرِينَ — And We made another people inherit them. (44:28). The pronoun refers to the omitted object of "they left," which was explained by His saying: مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ etc. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَما بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ السَّماءُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَما كانُوا مُنْظَرِينَ — So neither the sky nor the earth wept for them, nor were they granted any respite. (44:29). The weeping of the sky and the earth over a lost thing is an imaginative metaphor (كناية تخييلية) for their being affected by its loss and absence. Thus, the absence of their weeping over them after their destruction is a metaphor for the insignificance of their affair to Allah and the lack of impact of their destruction on any part of the cosmos.

His saying: وَما كانُوا مُنْظَرِينَ — nor were they granted any respite — is a metaphor for the swiftness of the execution of the Divine decree and Lordly subjugation regarding them, and that it encountered no obstacle to prevent it or require a remedy to remove it such that it would be delayed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ نَجَّيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ مِنَ الْعَذابِ الْمُهِينِ — Certainly We delivered the Children of Israel from the humiliating punishment, (44:30). This refers to what afflicted them while they were in the captivity of Pharaoh, such as the slaughtering of sons, the sparing of women, and other such things.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ كانَ عالِياً مِنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ — from Pharaoh. Indeed he was a tyrant, one of the transgressors. (44:31). مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ — from Pharaoh — is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: مِنَ الْعَذابِ — from the punishment — either by omitting a prefixed noun, the estimation being "from the punishment of Pharaoh," or without omission by making Pharaoh the essence of the punishment as a claim of hyperbole. His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ عالِياً مِنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ means he was arrogant, among the people of excess (الإسراف) and transgression beyond the limit.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدِ اخْتَرْناهُمْ عَلى عِلْمٍ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ — Certainly We chose them knowingly above all the nations, (44:32). Meaning: We chose them based on Our knowledge of their worthiness of choice, as the context implies.

"All the nations" (العالمين) refers to all the nations of the world if the choice intended is a choice from certain aspects, such as the abundance of prophets; for they are distinguished from other nations by the large number of prophets sent from among them, and they are distinguished by the long period they spent in the wilderness (التيه) shaded by clouds and eating manna and salwa, among other things.

Or it refers to the people of their own time if the choice intended is absolute; for they were not chosen over the Islamic nation (الأمة الإسلامية) whom Allah, the Exalted, addressed with the likes of His saying: كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ — You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind: (3:110), and His saying: هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ — He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in religion (22:78).

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْناهُمْ مِنَ الْآياتِ ما فِيهِ بَلؤُا مُبِينٌ — and We gave them such signs as contained a manifest test. (44:33). Trial (البلاء) is testing (الاختبار) and examination (الامتحان). Meaning: We gave the Children of Israel such miraculous signs as contained a manifest test. Indeed, they were given miraculous signs the likes of which were not known in other nations, and they were tested thereby with a manifest trial.

It is said: In His saying: فِيهِ — in it — there is an indication that there were other matters as well, such as its being a miracle.

In the conclusion of the story with these four verses—meaning His saying: وَلَقَدْ نَجَّيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ until His saying: بَلؤُا مُبِينٌ—there is a kind of consolation for the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and an allusion that Allah, the Exalted, will save him and the believers with him from the Pharaohs of Mecca, and will choose them and establish them in the land to see how they will act.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Jawami' al-Jami', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَارْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِي السَّماءُ بِدُخانٍ مُبِينٍ, there is a difference of opinion regarding the smoke. It is said: It is a smoke that comes from the sky before the establishment of the Hour—it enters the ears of the disbelievers—until the head of one of them becomes like a roasted head (الحنيذ), and the believer is affected by it like the state of a common cold—and the entire earth becomes like a house in which a fire has been lit—having no apertures (خصاص); this lasts for forty days. This has been narrated from Ali, Ibn Abbas, and al-Hasan.

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from them, and also from Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and it was also narrated from Ibn Umar as a suspended (موقوف) report.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the verse, he said: "That is when they emerge from the grave during the Return (الرجعة)—darkness enshrouds all the people—so they say: 'This is a painful punishment—Our Lord! Remove this punishment from us, for we are indeed believers!'"

In al-Majma', Zurarah ibn A'yan narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "The sky wept over Yahya ibn Zakariyya and al-Husayn ibn Ali (peace be upon them) for forty mornings." I asked: "What was its weeping?" He said: "It would rise red and set red."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ubayd al-Maktab from Ibrahim, who said: "The sky has not wept since the world began except over two." It was said to Ubayd: "Does not the sky and the earth weep over the believer?" He said: "That is his station and where his deeds ascend." He asked: "Do you know what the weeping of the sky is?" He said: "No." He said: "It turns red and becomes like a rose, like [red] oil (كالدهان). When Yahya ibn Zakariyya was killed, the sky turned red and dripped blood, and indeed, on the day al-Husayn ibn Ali was killed, the sky turned red."

In al-Faqih, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), he said: "When a believer dies, the places of the earth—in which he used to worship Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—weep over him, as does the gate through which his deeds used to ascend and the place of his prostration."

I say: Regarding this meaning and the meaning of the two previous narrations, there are other narrations from both Shi'i and Sunni chains.

If the meaning of the weeping of the sky and the earth were based on what appears from these narrations, there would be no need to interpret their weeping as an imaginative metaphor.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ, he said: "They said that when the revelation descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—and a state of fainting (الغشي) overcame him—so they said: 'He is mad.'"

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَيَقُولُونَ (٣٤)
These ones say, (34)
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُنشَرِينَ (٣٥)
‘It will be only our first death, and we shall not be resurrected. (35)
فَأْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٦)
Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (36)
أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٣٧)
Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘, and those who were before them? We destroyed them; indeed, they were guilty. (37)
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ (٣٨)
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. (38)
مَا خَلَقْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
We did not create them except with consummate wisdom; but most of them do not know. (39)
إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ مِيقَـٰتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٠)
Indeed the Day of Judgement is the tryst for them all, (40)
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى مَوْلًى عَن مَّوْلًى شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤١)
the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, (41)
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٤٢)
except for him on whom Allah has mercy. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (42)
إِنَّ شَجَرَتَ ٱلزَّقُّومِ (٤٣)
Indeed the tree of Zaqqum (43)
طَعَامُ ٱلْأَثِيمِ (٤٤)
will be the food of the sinful. (44)
كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَغْلِى فِى ٱلْبُطُونِ (٤٥)
Like molten copper it will boil in their bellies, (45)
كَغَلْىِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٦)
seething like boiling water. (46)
خُذُوهُ فَٱعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٤٧)
[The keepers of hell will be told,] ‘Seize him and drag him to the middle of hell, (47)
ثُمَّ صُبُّوا۟ فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٨)
then pour over his head boiling water as punishment, (48)
ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْكَرِيمُ (٤٩)
[and tell them,] “Taste this! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and noble! (49)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ (٥٠)
This is what you used to doubt!’ (50)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ (٥١)
Indeed the Godwary will be in a safe place, (51)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٥٢)
amid gardens and springs, (52)
يَلْبَسُونَ مِن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٥٣)
dressed in fine silk and brocade, sitting face to face. (53)
كَذَٰلِكَ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٥٤)
So shall it be, and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris. (54)
يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ ءَامِنِينَ (٥٥)
Secure [from any kind of harm,] there they will call for every kind of fruit [they wish]. (55)
لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ إِلَّا ٱلْمَوْتَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥٦)
Other than the first death, they will not taste death therein, and He will save them from the punishment of hell (56)
فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٥٧)
—a grace from your Lord. That is the great success. (57)
فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
Indeed We have made it simple in your language, so that they may take admonition. (58)
فَٱرْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُم مُّرْتَقِبُونَ (٥٩)
So wait! They [too] are waiting. (59)

(Commentary)

After warning the people of worldly punishment and then of the punishment of the Hereafter, and illustrating the worldly punishment through what befell the people of Pharaoh—when Moses (عليه السلام) brought them the message from his Lord, but they denied him, so Allah seized them with the punishment of drowning and eradicated them—

The discourse returns to the punishment of the Hereafter. It mentions the people's denial of the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of the soul to the body for judgment — and their claim that there is no life after the first death. He then argues for the proof of the Resurrection with logical demonstration (البرهان), followed by an announcement of some of what the criminals (المجرمون) will encounter of punishment in the Hereafter, and some of what the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who guard themselves against evil through piety and awareness of Allah — will encounter of everlasting bliss. At that point, the Surah concludes with what it began with: the revelation of the Book for the purpose of remembrance (التذكر), and the command to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to watch and wait.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَيَقُولُونَ إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى وَما نَحْنُ بِمُنْشَرِينَ "Indeed these say, 'It is nothing but our first death, and we shall not be resurrected.'" (44:34-35). This is a return to the beginning of the discourse from His saying: بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ "Rather they are in doubt, playing around." (44:9). The demonstration "these" (هؤلاء) refers to the Quraysh and those associated with them among the pagan Arabs who denied the Resurrection. Their saying: "It is nothing but our first death" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى) is intended to negate life after death, which necessitates the negation of the Resurrection, as evidenced by their subsequent saying: "and we shall not be resurrected" (وَما نَحْنُ بِمُنْشَرِينَ), meaning we shall not be raised. It is mentioned in al-Kashshaf: It is said, "Allah anshara (أنشر) the dead" or "nasharahum (نشرهم)" when He raises them. End quote.

Thus, their saying: "It is nothing but our first death" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى)—the pronoun "it" (هي) refers to the ultimate end (العاقبة) and the finality (النهاية). That is: the end of our affair and the finality of our existence and life is nothing but our first death; we perish by it and there is no life after it ever.

As for the reason for qualifying the death in the verse as "the first" (الأولى), it has been said that it is not a restrictive qualification (قيد احترازي), as there is no necessary correlation between the first and the last, or between the first and the second. It is possible for something to be "first" without there being a "second" for it, nor another in opposition to it.

There is another view mentioned by al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf, where he said: "If you ask: The discourse was regarding the second life, not death, so why was it not said: 'It is nothing but our first life, and we shall not be resurrected,' as it was said: 'It is nothing but our life in this world, and we shall not be raised'? And what is the meaning of His saying: 'except our first death' (إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى)? And what is the meaning of mentioning 'the first'? It is as if they were promised another death such that they negated and denied it while affirming the first."

"I say: Its meaning—and Allah is the Granter of success to what is right—is that they were told: 'Indeed you shall die a death followed by life, just as a death preceded you which was followed by life,' and that is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ '...and you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will cause you to die, then He will bring you to life...' (2:28). So they said: 'It is nothing but our first death,' meaning that the death which is characterized by being followed by life is nothing but the first death, not the second death. This attribute you describe death with—of life following it—belongs only to the first death specifically. Thus, there is no difference in meaning between this and His saying: 'It is nothing but our life in this world.'" End quote.

It is possible to explain it in a third way: that they said: "It is nothing but our first death" after hearing His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ "They will say, 'Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life...'" (40:11). It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse that the first causing of death is the death after the life of this world, and the second causing of death is that which occurs after the life of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection. Thus, in their saying: "It is nothing but our first death," they are negating the second death which is inseparable from the life of the intermediate realm, which is life after death; for they view the death of a human being as his annihilation (انعدام) and the nullification (بطلان) of his essence (الذات).

It is possible to explain it in a fourth way: that the qualification of "the first" (الأولى) refers to the narration (الحكاية) rather than the narrated content (المحكي). That is, what they actually said was: "It is nothing but our death," and the meaning of the discourse is that these people negate life after death and say: "It is nothing but our death," meaning the first death of the two deaths we mentioned in our saying: "They will say, 'Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die...'"

The four views differ in their proximity to understanding; the closest is the third, then the fourth, then the first.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ "Then bring back our fathers, if you are truthful." (44:36). This is the completion of the people's speech and a challenge from them to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers in him, as they used to mention the Resurrection (البعث) and the restoration of life (الإحياء) to them. They argued to reject the restoration of life after death by saying: "Then bring back our fathers, if you are truthful," meaning: let our past forefathers be brought back to life through your prayer or whatever means you have taken, so that we may know your truthfulness in your claim that the dead will be brought to life and that death is not annihilation.

His saying, the Exalted: أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ "Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘ and those who were before them? We destroyed them; indeed they were criminals." (44:37). This is a threat to the people of destruction (الإهلاك), just as the people of Tubba‘ and the nations before them were destroyed.

This Tubba‘ (تبع) was one of the kings of the Himyarites in Yemen, and his name, according to what has been mentioned, was As‘ad Abu Karib; it is also said: Sa‘d Abu Karib. A portion of his story will follow in the discussion of narrations (البحث الروائي). In the discourse, there is a subtle indication of the safety of Tubba‘ himself from destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with consummate wisdom, but most of them do not know." (44:38-39). The dual pronoun in His saying: "and whatever is between them" (وَما بَيْنَهُما) refers to the two genera of the heavens and the earth, which is why it was not pluralized. The preposition bi (بـ) in His saying "with truth" (بِالْحَقِّ) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة), meaning: We did not create them except as being clothed in truth. Some have allowed it to denote causality (السببية), meaning: We did not create them for any reason except for the sake of the Truth, which is faith, obedience, resurrection, and recompense; however, its remoteness is not hidden.

The content of the two verses is a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the establishment of the Resurrection. Its formulation is that if there were not beyond this world a stable and lasting world, but rather Allah continued to bring things into existence and then annihilate them, then bring other things into existence and annihilate them, giving life to this one and then causing him to die, and giving life to another, and so on—He would be a player (لاعب) in His action, acting in vain (عابث). Play is impossible for Him, the Exalted; rather, His action is truth (حق) and has a sound purpose. Therefore, there is another world, lasting and eternal, to which things transition. What is in this worldly, vanishing, and perishing realm is a prelude to the transition to that world, which is the life of the Hereafter.

We have detailed the discussion on this proof in the commentary of verse 16 of Surah al-Anbiya and verse 27 of Surah Sad, so let it be referred to.

His saying: "but most of them do not know" (وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ) is a reproach to them for their ignorance.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ "Indeed the Day of Judgment is the tryst for them all" (44:40). This is an explanation of the attribute of the Day established by the previous proof, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) wherein mankind will stand before the Lord of all the worlds.

Allah named it the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل) — the decisive day when truth is distinguished from falsehood — because on it, a separation is made between truth and falsehood, between the one on the right and the one on the wrong, and between the God-conscious and the criminals; or because it is the Day of the Decisive Judgment (القضاء الفصل) from Him, the Exalted.

His saying: "the tryst for them all" (مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ) means the appointed time for all people, or the appointed time for those previously mentioned: the people of Tubba‘, the people of Pharaoh, those before them, the Quraysh, and others.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ "the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped" (44:41). This is an explanation of the Day of Sorting. The mawla (المولى) is the companion who has the right to manage the affairs of his companion; it is applied both to the one who takes charge of an affair and the one whose affair is taken charge of. The first mawla in the verse is the former, and the second is the latter.

The verse first negates that a friend will avail his friend on that day, and secondly informs that they will not be helped. The difference between the two meanings is that "availing" (الإغناء) occurs when the one providing aid acts independently, and the one being aided has no part in it; whereas "help" (النصرة) occurs when the one being helped possesses some incomplete means of victory, and it is completed for him by the help of the helper.

The reason for the negation of availing and help on that day is that the causes (الأسباب) that are effective in the life of this world fall away on the Day of Resurrection. He, the Exalted, said: وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ "...and all their means are cut off" (2:166), and He said: فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ "...then We shall set them apart from one another" (10:28).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللهُ إِنَّهُ هُوة الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "except him on whom Allah has mercy. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (44:42). This is an exception from the pronoun in "nor will they be helped" (وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ). The verse is among the proofs for intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — on that day. The detailed discussion on intercession has already been presented in the first volume of this book.

This is based on the assumption that the pronoun in "nor will they be helped" refers to all people, as is apparent.

However, if it refers to the disbelievers (الكفار), as has been said, then the exception is disconnected (منقطع), and the meaning is: But those whom Allah has mercied—and they are the God-conscious—they are in no need of a friend to avail them or a helper to help them.

As for what some have allowed—that it is a connected exception (استثناء متصل) from "friend" (مَوْلًى)—its invalidity has become clear from what we have previously stated. For availing only occurs when a person possesses none of the means of salvation, and whoever is in this state, no one can avail him, nor is there any exception. Intercession is a form of help that requires some means of salvation, which is the pleasing religion (الدين المرضي), as previously mentioned in the discussion on intercession. Yes, it can be explained by what will come in the narration of al-Shahham.

His saying: "Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ) means the Overcomer (الغالب) whom nothing can overcome so as to prevent Him from punishing whom He wills to punish, and the Bestower of good upon whom He wills to have mercy. The suitability of these two Noble Names to the contents of the verses is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ "Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful." (44:43-44). The discussion on the tree of Zaqqum has already been presented in the commentary of Surah al-Saffat. The sinful (الأثيم) is the one in whom sin has become established, either by persistence in disobedience or by the multiplicity of sins. This verse, until the completion of eight verses, is an explanation of the state of the people of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: كَالْمُهْلِ يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ "Like molten copper, it will boil in their bellies, like the boiling of boiling water." (44:45-46). Molten metal (المهل) is melted copper, lead, or similar substances. Boiling (الغلي) and effervescence (الغليان) are well-known. Scalding water (الحميم) is water that is intensely hot. His saying: "Like molten metal" is a second predicate for "Indeed" (إِنَّ), just as His saying: "food of the sinful" is the first predicate. His saying: "it will boil in their bellies like the boiling of scalding water" is a third predicate. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: خُذُوهُ فَاعْتِلُوهُ إِلى سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ "[It will be said,] ‘Seize him and drag him into the midst of hell." (44:47). Dragging (الاعتلاء) is to shake and push with violence. The "midst of hell" (سواء الجحيم) is its center. The address is to the angels entrusted with the Fire, meaning: We say to the angels, "Take the sinful one and push him violently into the center of the Fire so that it surrounds him." He, the Exalted, said: وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ "...and indeed hell besieges the faithless" (9:49).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ صُبُّوا فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِ مِنْ عَذابِ الْحَمِيمِ "Then pour over his head the punishment of boiling water." (44:48). It seems that "punishment" (عذاب) refers to that by which one is punished, and its annexation to "scalding water" (الحميم) is explanatory (بيانية). The meaning is: Then pour over his head some of the scalding water by which he is punished.

His saying, the Exalted: ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْكَرِيمُ "‘Taste [this]! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and generous!" (44:49). This is an address directed to the sinful one while he is enduring punishment after punishment. Describing him with might (العزة) and nobility (الكرامة) despite his state of humiliation and baseness is a form of mockery (استهزاء) to intensify his punishment. He used to see for himself in the world a might and nobility that would never leave him, as appears from what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of his saying: وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى "...I do not think the Hour will ever set in, and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best with Him." (41:50).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذا ما كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَمْتَرُونَ "This is indeed what you used to doubt.’" (44:50). Doubting (الامتراء) is skepticism and suspicion. The verse is the completion of their saying to him: "Taste..." etc. In it is an emphasis and an informing of them of their error and slip in the world when they doubted what they today witness of the punishment with ocular vision. For this reason, the enduring of punishment was expressed as "tasting" (الذوق), because the complete perception of the pain of painful things and the pleasure of pleasurable things is expressed as tasting.

It is possible that the verse is a new sentence (استئناف) from the speech of Allah, the Glorified, addressing the disbelievers after mentioning their state on the Day of Resurrection. This might be supported by His saying: "you [plural] used to doubt" (كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَمْتَرُونَ) with a plural address, whereas the address in the previous verses was singular.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي مَقامٍ أَمِينٍ "Indeed the God-conscious will be in a safe station" (44:51). Station (المقام) is the place of standing in the sense of stability and rootedness; hence it is also interpreted as the place of residence. Safe (الأمين) is an attribute from security (الأمن) in the sense of not being afflicted by anything disliked. The meaning is: The God-conscious—on the Day of Resurrection—are stable in a place characterized by security from being afflicted by anything disliked at all.

From this, it becomes clear that attributing security to the "station" by describing the station as "safe" is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة).

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ "amid gardens and springs" (44:52). This is an explanation of His saying: "in a safe station." The springs were made a vessel (ظرف) for them by virtue of proximity and their existence in the gardens which are the vessel. The gardens were pluralized in consideration of the diversity of their types, or because each of them alone has a garden or more.

His saying, the Exalted: يَلْبَسُونَ مِنْ سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ "clad in silk and brocade, facing one another." (44:53). Silk (السندس) is fine silk, and brocade (الإستبرق) is thick silk; both are Arabized from Persian.

His saying: "facing one another" (مُتَقابِلِينَ) means they face each other for sociability, as there is no evil or disliked thing among them because they are in a safe station.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ "So shall it be, and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris." (44:54). Meaning: the matter is as such, i.e., as We have described. What is meant by "wedding them to houris" is making them companions to them, from zawj (الزوج) meaning companion (القرين), which is the linguistic root of marriage. Houris (الحور) is the plural of hawra’ (حوراء), meaning one with intense blackness and whiteness of the eye, or one with black pupils like gazelles. Large-eyed (العين) is the plural of ‘ayna’ (عيناء), meaning one with large eyes. The apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, is that the hur al-‘ayn are other than the women of the world who enter Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِكُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ آمِنِينَ "There they will call for every kind of fruit, in security." (44:55). Meaning: secure from its harm.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى وَوَقاهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ "They will not taste death therein, except the first death, and He will spare them the punishment of hell" (44:56). Meaning: they are in the Garden of Eternity, alive with an eternal life that death does not touch.

A problem has been raised regarding the verse: that the exception of the first death from His saying: "They will not taste death therein" implies that they taste the first death therein, whereas the opposite is certainly intended. In another formulation: the first death is the death of the world, and it has already passed for the people of Paradise. To be characterized in the future by a past matter is certainly impossible; so what is the meaning of excepting the first death from the absence of tasting in the future?

There is another problem they have not addressed: it has already been mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ "Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life" (40:11), that between the life of this world and the Hour there are two deaths: a death by transition from the world to the intermediate realm, and a death by transition from the intermediate realm to the Hereafter. It is apparent that what is meant by "the first death" in the verse is the death of the world that transfers man to the intermediate realm. Suppose we resolved the exception of the first death in some way; why then was the second death not excepted? What is the difference between them when they are two deaths they tasted before entering the Garden of Eternity?

The answer to the first problem is that the exception is disconnected (منقطع), and the meaning is: But they have already tasted the first death in the world and it has passed, so the generality of His saying: "They will not taste death therein" remains as it is.

On the assumption that the exception is not disconnected, "except" (إلا) means "other than" (سوى), and "except the first death" (إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى) is a substitute (بدل) for "death" (الْمَوْتَ) and is not part of the exception at all. The meaning is: They will not taste therein any death other than the first death. As for the first death, they have already tasted it, and it is impossible for it to return and be tasted, as it is the first.

Other answers have been given that are not worth considering. You are aware that neither of the two views explains the characterization of the death as "the first," and several views on this have already been presented in the commentary of His saying: "It is nothing but our first death."

As for the second problem, it can be answered by the second answer mentioned above, since there are two deaths: the first death, which transfers man from the world to the intermediate realm, and the second death, which transfers him from the intermediate realm to the Hereafter. Thus, if "except" (إِلَّا) in His saying: "except the first death" means "other than," and the whole phrase is a substitute for "death," the verse is intended to negate anything other than the first death, which is the second death—the death of the intermediate realm. Thus, there is no death in the Garden of the Hereafter: not the death of the world, because it was realized for them previously, nor anything other than the death of the world, which is the death of the intermediate realm. By this, the reason for qualifying the death as "the first" becomes clear.

His saying: "and He will spare them the punishment of hell" (وَوَقاهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ). Protection (الوقاية) is to preserve a thing from what hurts and harms it. The meaning is: He preserved them from the punishment of hell. Mentioning their protection from the punishment of hell along with the negation of death from them is a completion of the division of disliked things. That is: they are protected from transitioning from one abode to another and from the state of Paradise to another state—which is death—and they are protected from transitioning from a happy state to a wretched state—which is the punishment of hell.

His saying, the Exalted: فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكَ ذلِكَ هُوة الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ "as a bounty from your Lord. That is the great success." (44:57). This is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for what was previously mentioned of honor and blessing. It could also be an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) or an object of purpose (مفعول له). In any case, it is a favor (تفضل) from Him, the Exalted, without any entitlement (استحقاق) from the servants—an entitlement that would necessitate or obligate Him to reward. For He, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) who is not owned; nothing can dictate to Him. It is only His promise to His servants, and then He informed that He does not break His promise. The detailed discussion of this meaning has already been presented in previous sections.

His saying: "That is the great success" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ). Success (الفوز) is the attainment of what is desired, and its being a "great success" is because it is the ultimate happiness a human being can achieve.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِنَّما يَسَّرْناهُ بِلِسانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ "Indeed We have made it simple in your language so that they may take admonition." (44:58). This is a derivation (تفريع) from all that has preceded from the beginning of the Surah until here, and a summary (فذلكة) of the whole. Simplification (التيسير) is making easy. The pronoun refers to the Book, and what is meant by the Prophet's language (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is Arabic.

The meaning is: We have only made the Qur'an easy—meaning the understanding of its objectives—through the Arabic language so that they—meaning your people—might take admonition. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ "Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (43:3).

It has been said: The meaning of simplifying the Book in the Prophet's language (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is making it flow upon his tongue while he was unlettered (أمي), neither reading nor writing, so that it might be a sign (آية) of the truthfulness of his prophethood. This is remote from the context of the summary.

His saying, the Exalted: فَارْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُمْ مُرْتَقِبُونَ "So watch! They [too] are watching." (44:59). It is as if this is derived from what is derived from the previous verse. The sum of the meaning is: We simplified it in Arabic in the hope that they would take admonition, but they did not; rather, they are in doubt, playing around, and waiting for the punishment that cannot be averted from the deniers. So wait for the punishment; indeed, they are waiting for it.

Thus, applying the term "watchers" (المرتقبين) to the people is by way of sarcasm (تهكم). Among the weak opinions is the saying of those who claim that in the verse is a command to leave them alone, and that it was abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of the sword.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ "Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘..." (44:37), Sahl ibn Sa‘id narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: لا تسبوا تبعا فإنه كان قد أسلم — "Do not revile Tubba‘, for he had indeed embraced Islam."

I say: This meaning was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn ‘Abbas, and also from Ibn ‘Asakir from ‘Ata’ ibn Abi Rabah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, al-Walid ibn Sabih narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) that he said: "Indeed Tubba‘ said to the Aws and the Khazraj: 'Stay here until this Prophet comes forth; as for me, if I were to reach him, I would serve him and go forth with him.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu‘aym narrated in al-Dala’il from ‘Abdullah ibn Salam who said: "Tubba‘ did not die until he believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—when the Jews of Yathrib used to inform him."

I say: The reports regarding the matter of Tubba‘ are many, and in some of them, it is mentioned that he was the first to clothe the Kaaba.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Zayd al-Shahham, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said to me while we were on the road on a Friday night: 'Recite some Qur'an, for it is Friday night.' So I recited: إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ—يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً—وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللهُ 'Indeed the Day of Judgment is the tryst for them all—the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, except him on whom Allah has mercy.' Then Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: 'By Allah, we are the ones whom Allah excepted—so we used to avail them.'"

I say: He (عليه السلام) is referring to intercession (الشفاعة), and he took the exception as referring to the first "friend" (مولى).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Then He said: إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ "Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful." (44:43-44). It was revealed concerning Abu Jahl ibn Hisham. Regarding His saying: كَالْمُهْلِ "Like molten metal," he said: "Molten metal is melted brass." يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ "it will boil in their bellies like the boiling of boiling water"—and it is that which has been heated and reached the ultimate limit.

I say: From the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah as well, there are narrations that support the revelation of the verse concerning Abu Jahl.
